coredata.dc:title: Hydroxyl edge-functionalized graphene quantum dots for gas-sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Arunragsa, Sarun
Seekaew, Yotsarayuth
Pon-On, Weeraphat
Wongchoosuk, Chatchawal
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an alternative room-temperature gas sensor was fabricated by drop-casting hydroxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs) onto an interdigitated nickel electrode. The OH-GQDs were prepared based on a bottom-up approach using the hydrothermal treatment of pyrene. Physicochemical characterizations based on the FE-TEM, UV–vis, Raman and XPS confirmed the successful formation of OH-GQDs with high homogeneity and purity. The gas-sensing mechanism of OH-GQDs gas sensor was systematically investigated via gas-sensing experiments and self-consistent charge density functional tight-binding (SCC-DFTB) calculations. The results showed that the room-temperature OH-GQDs gas sensor owns high sensitivity and selectivity as well as fast response to ammonia (NH3) with linear relationship at the concentration ranging from 10 to 500 ppm. The SCC-DFTB calculations indicated that the OH functional group was a major contributing factor to the NH3 sensitivity and selectivity. This study revealed that the modification of GQDs via edge functionalization is a promising approach to attain high performance gas sensors with high selectivity to a desired target gas.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hydroxyl functional group
Ammonia gas sensor
Pyrene
SCC-DFTB

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of hydroxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs)
Fabrication of hydroxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots gas sensor
The sensing mechanism of OH-GQDs gas sensors
NH3 adsorption on OH-GQDs based on SCC-DFTB

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Kasetsart University Research and Development Institute

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of OH-GQDs synthesis and the fabrication process of OH-GQDs gas sensors (bottom left).
UV–visible spectra of OH-GQDs (red line) and pyrene (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS spectra of OH-GQDs sensing film: (a) survey scan, (b) the high-resolution C 1s and (c) the high-resolution O 1s. (d) Raman spectrum of OH-GQDs sensing film.
(a) TEM image and (b) size distribution of synthesized OH-GQDs.
Changes in resistance of OH-GQDs gas sensor to ammonia vapor with various concentrations at room temperature.
Selectivity histogram of the OH-GQDs gas sensor to various gases at room temperature.

Models of interaction between OH-GQDs and NH3 molecule via (a) N atom and (b) H atom of NH3 molecule absorbed (I) in the middle, (II) at a carbon atom and (III) on an edge of OH-GQDs. (c) GQDs-NH3 interaction energies with different adsorption sites and configurations as a function of the adsorption distance.

head.ce:title: Hydroxyl edge-functionalized graphene quantum dots for gas-sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok 10900, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok 10900 Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graphene and Printed Electronics Research Team (GPE), National Science and Technology Development Agency (NSTDA), 111 Thailand Science Park, Phahon Yothin Road, Klong Nueng, Klong Luang, Phathum Thani 12120, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graphene and Printed Electronics Research Team (GPE)
National Science and Technology Development Agency (NSTDA)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graphene and Printed Electronics Research Team (GPE), National Science and Technology Development Agency (NSTDA), 111 Thailand Science Park, Phahon Yothin Road, Klong Nueng, Klong Luang, Phathum Thani 12120, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an alternative room-temperature gas sensor was fabricated by drop-casting hydroxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs) onto an interdigitated nickel electrode. The OH-GQDs were prepared based on a bottom-up approach using the hydrothermal treatment of pyrene. Physicochemical characterizations based on the FE-TEM, UV–vis, Raman and XPS confirmed the successful formation of OH-GQDs with high homogeneity and purity. The gas-sensing mechanism of OH-GQDs gas sensor was systematically investigated via gas-sensing experiments and self-consistent charge density functional tight-binding (SCC-DFTB) calculations. The results showed that the room-temperature OH-GQDs gas sensor owns high sensitivity and selectivity as well as fast response to ammonia (NH3) with linear relationship at the concentration ranging from 10 to 500 ppm. The SCC-DFTB calculations indicated that the OH functional group was a major contributing factor to the NH3 sensitivity and selectivity. This study revealed that the modification of GQDs via edge functionalization is a promising approach to attain high performance gas sensors with high selectivity to a desired target gas.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene discovered by Novoselov et al. in 2004 [1] has become one of the most popular two-dimensional (2D) materials because of its unique properties including single atomic layer thickness, high thermal conductivity, remarkable room-temperature electron mobility, zero-band-overlap semimetal character, and exceptional tensile strength [2–7]. Up to now, graphene has been applied in various technological applications such as batteries [8], supercapacitors [9], solar cells [10], biotechnology [11] and electronics [12]. In particular, the tunable bandgap property of graphene can enable new functionalities for various applications especially electronics. It has been extensively studied theoretically and experimentally by introducing defects into graphene and controlling the lateral dimension of graphene within the quantum scale (less than 30 nm) to form graphene quantum dots (GQD) [13–16]. GQDs combine the advantages of graphene and quantum dots associated with 2D and quantum confinement effects leading to high optical transparency, high surface area, distinctive photoluminescence, low toxicity, chemical stability and tunable energy band gap [16–22]. Very recently, there has been increasing interest in the synthesis of GQDs and their applications such as perovskite solar cells [23], supercapacitor [24], biosensors [25], electrochemiluminescence sensors [26] and lithium ion batteries [27]. However, gas-sensing application based on GQDs is still limited. GQDs can generally be synthesized by top-down and bottom-up strategies [16]. Although the top-down approach based on exfoliation graphite of into graphene sheets by acidic oxidation [28], solvothermal [29,30], microwave-assisted [31] and electrochemical [32] methods have been extensively applied to synthesize GQDs due to their simplicity and low cost, they seriously suffer from poor control of GQDs' size and purity. The bottom-up techniques that build GQDs from small organic molecules via fusion and carbonization processes are potential means to overcome the shortcoming [33].

In the present work, hydroxyl-functionalized GQDs (OH-GQDs) were synthesized based on a bottom-up approach using the hydrothermal treatment of pyrene and investigated for ammonia (NH3) gas-sensing applications. The OH-GQDs gas sensor was fabricated by drop casting method, and characterized by various methods to evaluate their homogeneity, purity and gas-sensing performances. The fabricated OH-GQDs gas sensor showed high sensitivity and selectivity to NH3 at room temperature. In addition, the gas-sensing mechanisms of OH-GQDs gas sensor were proposed and discussed with the analysis of NH3 adsorption on OH-GQDs using the simulation based on density functional tight-binding (DFTB) method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hydroxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The OH-GQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal treatment of pyrene [34] as displayed in Fig. 1 . Briefly, 0.5 g of pyrene was nitrated in hot nitric acid (40 ml) at 80 °C under stirring for 12 h. After removing the acid by mixing with deionized (DI) water and filtering through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, the resultant 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was hydrothermal treated in 0.2 M NaOH aqueous solution at 200 °C for 10 h. The OH-GQDs solution was then purified in a dialysis bag for 2 days, and dried at 80 °C to obtain the dark brown OH-GQDs powder.

The absorption spectra of the synthesized samples were taken in the absorption wavelength range of 200–600 nm using Perkin Elmer lambda 650 UV/Vis spectrophotometer. Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscopy (FE-TEM, JEOL model: JEM-3100 (HR)) was used to investigate the morphology of OH-GQDs. The size distribution of OH-GQDs was determined by analyzing TEM images using Image J software. The elemental composition and chemical states of OH-GQDs were evaluated using Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The chemical bonding information of OH-GQDs was analyzed using a Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw model: Invia) operated with the laser excitation wavelength of 785 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of hydroxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots gas sensor
ce:section.ce:para: For the fabrication of gas sensors, a small amount (5 μl) of OH-GQDs solution (1 mg/ml) was dropped via a micropipette onto interdigitated Ni electrodes as shown in the inset of Fig. 1. The interdigitated Ni electrodes (0.5 μm thickness, 100 μm interdigit spacing and 4 × 4 mm2 electrode area) were prefabricated on an alumina substrate by standard photolithography, radio frequency sputtering and lift-off processes. After drop casting of the OH-GQDs solution, the sensor was kept at room temperature (26 ± 2 °C) for 24 h.

The gas-sensing properties of OH-GQDs gas sensors were measured by a standard flow-through system towards gases/volatile organic compounds including NH3, O2, ethanol, methanol, formalin, acetone, toluene and dimethylformamide (DMF). A constant flux of synthetic air was mixed with the target gas source to different concentrations ranging from 10 ppm to 500 ppm at room temperature (26 ± 2 °C) using mass flow controllers. The total gas flow rate was fixed at 1000 sccm. The gas sensor was exposed to a gas sample for 3 min at each gas concentrations and the air flux was restored for 5 min. All of the data in this experiment were recorded every second using our developed LabVIEW program via a USB DAQ device. The gas-sensing performances were evaluated in terms of gas response and selectivity. The sensor response is define as
(1)
S
%
=
R air
−
R gas
/
R air
× 100

where Rair and Rgas are the sensor resistance in clean air and target gas, respectively. The selectivity is the relative response compared between different gases. In addition, the response and recovery time were defined as the times required for the sensor resistance to reach 90% of the final value upon gas exposure and air recovery, respectively.

Self-consistent charge density functional tight-binding (SCC-DFTB) method is an approximate calculation technique based on DFT through a second order expansion of the DFT total energy with respect to charge density fluctuations (Δρ) relative to a chosen reference density (ρ 0) [35]. The total energy is expressed as a sum of three terms according to
(2)
E
SCC − DFTB
=
∑ iμv
c μ i
c v i
H μv 0
ρ 0
+
∑
A > B
E AB rep
ρ 0
+
1 2
∑ AB
γ AB
∆ q
A
∆ q
B

In short, it can be written as
(3)
E
SCC − DFTB
=
E BS
+
E rep
+
E
2 nd

The optical property of as-prepared OH-GQDs was measured by UV–visible spectrometer. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of OH-GQDs (Fig. 2 ) exhibits two absorption peaks at 239 nm and 357 nm with a tail across the entire visible region, which are similar to GQDs prepared by other methods [18,41–43]. These peaks can be related to π electron transitions in oxygen-containing GQDs. The shoulder peak at 239 nm is attributed to π-π* transition of CC, and the peak at 357 nm corresponds to n-π* transition of the CO bond. The OH-GQDs aqueous solution shows a dark brown color in day light and emits a yellow-green light (560 nm) when excited with a 400 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2a and b). The stability of synthesized OH-GQDs in aqueous solution was also investigated and the result demonstrated no sign of aggregation at room temperature for 6 months.

The XPS survey and core-level spectra of OH-GQDs are shown in Fig. 3a–c. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the XPS survey spectrum of OH-GQDs shows a predominant graphitic C 1s and O 1s peak. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) is composed of two peaks at 285.0 eV and 288.6 eV, specifying the presence of CC and COH, respectively [34,44,45]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum of the OH-GQDs (Fig. 3c) displays a single peak at 531.4 eV, corresponding to the hydroxyl group (OH). The results indicate that GQDs contain the hydroxyl functional group.

The Raman spectrum of OH-GQDs in the spectral range of 1100–1800 cm−1 is displayed in Fig. 3d. The spectrum shows typical graphitic features including D and G bands positioned at ~1350 and ~1580 cm−1, respectively. The D band originates from a breathing k-point A1g phonon related to edge states and defects while the G band corresponds to primary sp2-hybridized carbon bonds from degenerated E2g phonon mode in the Brillouin zone center [46,47]. The relative D/G band intensity was found to be ~0.82, indicating a high level of edge defects in the synthesized GQDs [48]. In addition, the D* band located at ~1265 cm−1 can be ascribed to COOH or COH edge functional groups [48,49] in accordance with the XPS analysis results.

The morphology of synthesized OH-GQDs characterized by TEM is displayed in Fig. 4 . It clearly shows approximately circular dots dispersed quite uniformly on an amorphous carbon layer of a TEM grid. The size distribution histogram as presented in Fig. 4b revealed that the sizes of synthesized OH-GQDs are varied in the range of 2–9 nm with the mean value of ~5 nm. It demonstrates that the pyrene precursor can offer relatively small sizes of GQDs over other carbon precursors. The high-resolution TEM image in inset of Fig. 4a illustrates a single crystalline GQD with an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) graphitic plane [18,34,41]. Furthermore, the result indicates no sp3 defect in the graphene basal plane suggesting that the hydroxyl groups of OH-GQDs are mostly bonded at edge-sites. It can signify the successful edge-functionalization of the OH group on GQDs.

Fig. 5 displays the dynamic response of OH-GQDs towards various concentrations of NH3 ranging from 10 to 500 ppm at room temperature (26 ± 2 °C) with 57 ± 2%RH. It is obvious that the sensor exhibits good reversibility of response towards NH3 pulses with various concentrations at room temperature. Upon exposure to NH3, the sensor resistance decreases rapidly and quickly recover to the initial value upon the removal of NH3 in air. The response behaviors may be attributed to the adsorption and desorption of NH3 molecules on the sensing films. The detail of the sensing mechanism of the OH-GQDs gas sensor will be further discussed in the next section. The gas responses of the OH-GQDs sensor towards NH3 at 500, 400, 300, 200, 100, 50, 10 ppm are calculated to be 76.63, 55.78, 38.98, 30.00, 14.16, 5.77 and 1.54%, respectively. From the inset plot in Fig. 5, the gas response of the OH-GQDs sensor increases approximately linearly over the NH3 concentration range of 10–500 ppm. The linear sensitivity of the OH-GQDs gas sensor obtained from the linear regression line in the inset of Fig. 5 is 0.1459 ppm−1. The calculated response and recovery times of the OH-GQDs sensor at 500 ppm NH3 are 64 and 69 s, respectively. Hence, the OH-GQDs sensor exhibits high NH3 sensitivity with short response and recovery times.

It is well known that high selectivity is an important property of a good gas sensor. Therefore, the selectivity of the OH-GQDs gas sensor has been evaluated by exposure to various environmental gases and volatile organic compound vapors including O2 (95%), formalin (CH2O), ethanol (C2H6O), methanol (CH3OH), toluene (C7H8), acetone (C3H6O), dimethylformamide (DMF) and ammonia (NH3) with a concentration of 500 ppm at room temperature as reported in Fig. 6 . It can be seen that the OH-GQDs gas sensor exhibits the highest response to NH3, indicating its high NH3 selectivity. The high selectivity towards NH3 may be attributed to the polar interaction between NH3 molecules and hydroxyl groups on OH-GQDs surface that can lead to stronger physisorptions compared with other tested gases, which may not be able to interact with the hydroxyl groups on the surfaces of OH-GQDs at room temperature [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of OH-GQDs gas sensors
ce:section.ce:para:
The gas-sensing mechanism of OH-GQDs may generally be ascribed to the adsorption and desorption of gas species, which act as electron donors or acceptors on the surfaces and edges of OH-GQDs. After NH3 molecules are adsorbed through the physical adsorption process, the resistance of OH-GQDs decreases before increasing to its initial baseline in the air. The resistance change behavior may be explained based on two possible mechanisms:
1)
Direct charge transfer between NH3 and OH-GQDs. When the sensor is exposed to NH3, NH3 molecules may adsorb on OH-GQDs sensing film at possible sites including edges and basal carbon atoms of OH-GQDs with different configurations via physisorption/chemisorption. It is well known that graphene, reduced graphene oxide, and GQDs mostly exhibit p-type conducting behaviors [51–54] but sometimes they can display n-type conductivity. For example, Bing et al. [55] found that the electronic and transport properties of the graphene nanoribbons with armchair-shaped edges exhibited an n-type sensing behavior after NH3 adsorption. In addition, Takatoshi et al. [56] reported that the KOH-treated bilayer graphene showed an n-type conduction property. In the present case, the resistance of OH-GQDs sensor decreases in the presence of NH3 and increases upon exposure to O2, a well-known electron withdrawing gas as shown in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary information. Thus, the OH-GQDs exhibit n-type conductivity. The electron transfer from the electron donating NH3 molecules to n-type OH-GQDs leads to an increase of the electron carrier density and a decrease of resistance. This proposed mechanism will be justified via SCC-DFTB simulation in the next section.
Direct charge transfer between NH3 and OH-GQDs. When the sensor is exposed to NH3, NH3 molecules may adsorb on OH-GQDs sensing film at possible sites including edges and basal carbon atoms of OH-GQDs with different configurations via physisorption/chemisorption. It is well known that graphene, reduced graphene oxide, and GQDs mostly exhibit p-type conducting behaviors [51–54] but sometimes they can display n-type conductivity. For example, Bing et al. [55] found that the electronic and transport properties of the graphene nanoribbons with armchair-shaped edges exhibited an n-type sensing behavior after NH3 adsorption. In addition, Takatoshi et al. [56] reported that the KOH-treated bilayer graphene showed an n-type conduction property. In the present case, the resistance of OH-GQDs sensor decreases in the presence of NH3 and increases upon exposure to O2, a well-known electron withdrawing gas as shown in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary information. Thus, the OH-GQDs exhibit n-type conductivity. The electron transfer from the electron donating NH3 molecules to n-type OH-GQDs leads to an increase of the electron carrier density and a decrease of resistance. This proposed mechanism will be justified via SCC-DFTB simulation in the next section.
2)
Reducing reaction between NH3 and chemisorbed oxygen (O 2(ads) + e − → O 2 −(ads)) on the n-type GQDs surface. In air, oxygen species (O 2 −) chemisorbed on the surface of OH-GQDs sensing film before NH3 gas exposure. When exposure to NH3 vapor, NH3 molecules will react with oxygen species and give electrons to the n-type OH-GQDs according to the reaction [57]:
(4)
4
NH 3
gas
+ 5
O 2 −
ads
→ 4 NO
gas
+ 6
H 2
O
gas
+ 5
e −
This process leads to an increase of electron density in the n-type OH-GQDs and a decrease of the sensor resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NH3 adsorption on OH-GQDs based on SCC-DFTB
ce:section.ce:para:
To support the proposed NH3-sensing mechanism, the SCC-DFTB simulation with van der Waals dispersion corrections was applied to investigate the adsorption behavior of NH3 molecule on OH-GQD gas sensors with different configurations as shown in Fig. 7 . The interaction energies (E) is defined as
(5)
E =
E
tot
NH 3
+ OH − GQDs
−
E
tot
OH − GQDs
−
E
tot
NH 3
where E
tot(NH 3+OH−GQDs), E
tot(OH−GQDs) and E
tot(NH 3) are the total energy of the OH-GQDs system with an adsorbed NH3 molecule, the OH-GQDs structure and a NH3 molecule, respectively.

The simulation model of armchair-edge GQDs functionalized with hydroxyl groups containing 114 carbon atoms with 30 armchair edges was designed according to the typical GQD size (~5 nm) based on the TEM data. All models were fully optimized based on SCC-DFTB calculations with the conjugate gradient method. Convergence criteria were a force threshold of 1 × 10−4 Ha/Bohr and a SCC tolerance of 1 × 10−5 Ha. The simulation models of NH3 adsorptions on an edge, in the middle and at a carbon atom of OH-GQDs with two different NH3 orientations are displayed in Fig. 7a and b, respectively. To find the most favorable adsorption configuration, an NH3 molecule is set at various distances (d) above the OH-GQDs. Fig. 7c shows the plot of interaction energy as a function of the distance (d). When the nitrogen atom of NH3 molecules oriented towards OH-GQDs (Fig. 7a), the highest adsorption energies on the edge, in the middle and at a carbon atom of OH-GQDs are −0.1948 eV, −0.1220 eV and −0.1178 eV at the distance of 2.0 Å, 3.2 Å and 3.2 Å, respectively. If the hydrogen atom of NH3 molecules oriented towards OH-GQDs (Fig. 7b), the highest adsorption energies on the edge, in the middle and at a carbon atom of OH-GQDs are −0.0586 eV, −0.1275 eV and −0.1236 eV at the distance of 2.2 Å, 2.6 Å and 2.6 Å, respectively. The calculated results suggest that the NH3 molecules can adsorb on OH-GQDs at all adsorption sites by physisorption. However, the NH3 molecules are weakly bound to the middle of OH-GQDs and prefer to adsorb on OH-GQDs via the functional group on an edge. The N atoms of NH3 tend to orient towards the OH functional groups at the distance of 2.0 Å. At this adsorption site, electron charge transfer would be from the NH3 molecule to the OH-GQD (0.022 e) in accordance with the experimental results and the proposed direct charge transfer sensing mechanism.

In conclusion, OH-GQDs were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of pyrene in alkaline aqueous solutions and systematically characterized for ammonia sensing at room temperature. UV–visible spectroscopy, XPS, Raman and HR-TEM characterizations confirmed that the synthesized structures were GQDs having the average diameter of ~5 nm functionalized with OH groups. From gas-sensing results, the OH-GQDs sensor exhibited high response towards ammonia in the concentration range of 10–500 ppm and high selectivity against CH2O, C2H6O, CH3OH, C7H8, C3H6O and DMF at room temperature. The structural and electronic properties of the OH-GQDs with ammonia adsorption were studied using SCC-DFTB simulation. The results indicated that the N atom of ammonia molecule exhibited the highest interaction on the edge of OH-GQDs and suggested an important role of OH functional groups at the edge of GQDs on the NH3 sensing mechanism at room temperature. Therefore, GQDs with effective functional groups could be valuable platforms for high performance room-temperature gas sensors.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Change in resistance of OH-GQDs gas sensor to O2 (95%) at room temperature upon 4 cycles.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2020.107790.

Sarun Arunragsa: Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Data curation. Yotsarayuth Seekaew: Formal analysis. Weeraphat Pon-On: Formal analysis. Chatchawal Wongchoosuk: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by a grant (TRF-KURDI Research Career Development Grant) from Thailand Research Fund and Kasetsart University Research and Development Institute (RSA6180062). S.A. acknowledges the scholarship under the Strengthening and Developing New Researcher Plan in conformance with the Research and Innovation of Graduate Study Strategy of the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) as of the fiscal year 2019 and the 50th Anniversary Scholarship from Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University.
